author: @TheRealMrLaserCutter
text: What's the reason for these types of batteries not being more common place? Why is it also convention to quote the energy density of the active material, if the active material is such a small part of the battery as a whole then should you not compare the whole system.

author: @perlygatekeeper
text:
When you compare your first test to lead acid for watt-hours/kilogram, what are you measuring for each?  It sounded like you were only accounting for the coating for your sample (the tire-originated carbon ink and not the steel mesh and electrode), where the lead-acid sounds like a watt-hours/kilogram for an entire battery; lead, electrolyte, electrodes, case, labels, etc.

Could you explain this clearly?

Very interested in your work, and really enjoying your videos.

author: @everest3334
text:
did gary apply to the newest 21 april 2017   https://www.gov.uk/government/news/business-secretary-announces-industrial-strategy-challenge-fund-investments they increased the investment a lot since the initial 2016 announcement i linked earlier...

"clean and flexible energy or the ‘Faraday Challenge’: an investment of £246 million over 4 years to help UK businesses seize the opportunities presented by the transition to a low carbon economy, to ensure the UK leads the world in the design, development and manufacture of batteries for the electrification of vehicles..."

"manufacturing and future materials: through a new £26 million fund for research and development programmes, the government will support the UK’s civil aerospace industry, a sector which employs over 230,000 people, to develop the next generation of affordable lightweight composite materials for aerospace, automotive and other advanced manufacturing sectors..." etc, etc

as another side note see https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=VEh7V9_uIqM
Orkney 2. Building the Future | Fully Charged Published on Sep 13, 2017

author: @Khwartz
text:
Hello Robert. Thanks for your reply and sorry for the delay of mine :/ I VERY// Understand your reasons while I think that Open Source
Engineering is COUNTER-productive respect to the Ecological aspect. I explain:The cost to create even a little unit of manufacture with the machines, the employed, the time to reach Enough Quality Production and have homologations, is in SEVERAL HUNDREDS OF THOUSANDS IF NOT A MILLION of Euros at least. Who, in the Industry, will risk to invest such money without protecting their investments by a Licence? if not Chineses because they don't really care with their huge world-while distribution systems... ^^ I thought I could have a little machine at my workshop and produce the cells but even if it would be possible (and maybe it is indeed, still have to counter-check that point) to have a little machine to do so, would be few k€, just the homologations are already in k€ and are VERY// hard to obtain, at least in France (reasons of security and viability: cells should not blow or burn and meet the requirements of durability). So for now, I'm merely stuck. Anyway, would be Just For My Own Pleasure, I am still Very Eager To Visit You but I am afraid that unless we associate with one of the big sharks companies of power cells, we won't have the financial to sustain the investment needed to go on Real Meaningful Production :( Best Regards, Didier. (couldn't replay in our thread of 4 weeks ago, my comment was always appearing in an other tread! lol so I have copy-pasted it here ;-) )

author: @davegeorge7094
text:
Is the low conductivity of stainless mesh causing heat problems? Did you plate/surround the SS with something more conductive? Are you using SS because it resists corrosion?
Please try I'm missing many tech words, frustrating, my volume is maxed out. I would try a external 20 buck corded desk mic, really simple. Thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not realy - yes and yes - the ss mesh is carbon coated and iuse it because it resisits corrosion as for the mic i use a zoom h2n the problem isn't the mic it's because this is a window into the lab not a recording studio

author: @charlheynike9619
text: Do you take into account the total weight or just the weight of active material when quoting energy density?

replies.author: @charlheynike9619
replies.text: What do you think is the best results so far if you consider total weight? Like the scooter battery for instance?

replies.author: @Khwartz
replies.text: hello Mate, I remember You that Ah/kg are not really "energy density" if the voltage is not specified; needs Wh/kg ;-)

author: @Khwartz
text: Hello again! Mate. I just "can't believe" the Amount of AWESOMENESS You Accumulate, All Of You Guys! :D So Very Very Nice And Great Results Again! "Can't wait" to visit You soon to see how I can help and how You can help me to spread this Green Technology in my country and maybe others! :) <3 <3 <3 Love You, guys! :p

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are more than welcome to visit anytime mate

replies.author: @Khwartz
replies.text: Robert, about the cost You announce, isn't it just the cost of the raw materials? I mean, this cost doesn't yet take in account the cost by year of the machinerie to manufacture them, nor the time to manage the production of the cells, nor I guess the containers chosen to contain the Active Material and Electrodes.  I've tried to find data about this kind of cost of actual present batteries but with no Success; would You have any idea of what would be the cos GLOBAL Manufacturing Cost of a 18650 type cell? and if possible: function of course of the amount of cells produced by year? please.

replies.author: @Khwartz
replies.text:
All Right, understand Mate. I will try to estimate that there with You if I can come to visit You (I've sent a mail to Gary about that ;-) ).

An other detail: I need to estimate the ecological impact of the cells. For this, I need to know the elementary components to check if they are safe to produce (the idea is to have cells which are "green"). I know there is carbon, stainless steal, what about the electrolytes or any other component? ^^

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I can't tell you that mate - not without an NDA and then it is highly unlikely unless we have a contract with you. That's just not info i will share so someone can asses a possibility mate - you have to understand that

author: @Venturestarx
text: A perfect use for the "tire battery" is for large scale power in homes and the like. It's cheep, easy, and enough energy density to do so. You could also look into the City power management applications, because it is far superior than the Vanadium cell devices that are currently being used.

author: @deemdoubleu
text: You quote energy density in Wh/Kg.  Is it useful to correct for the density of the battery material?  i.e. Wh/m3?  I only say this because although you compared the performance to a lead acid battery, the latter is quite compact (because it is heavy) and in some applications, it is not so much the weight but the volume which may be restricted.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i usse specific anargy a lot mate  (Wh/kg) because it is an easy metric for me - easier to weight than estimate volume - but you are right energy density (Wh/l) is useful - I am most interested in home storage and grid where volume is not so much of an issue

author: @TheKindHuman
text: I'm really excited for you guys. Can't wait until you get into commercial production. Once you get your top end battery sorted out I'm sure that Tesla (the car company) would be very interested. Your battery would instantly double the car range and improve performance with the lighter weight per KWH. Imagine an electric car with a range of 800 to 1000 mile range. Fantastic.

author: @billkillernic
text: Doesnt smearing their surface with your fingers change their properties drastically? When you hold the sheets towards the camera there seem to be great areas with varying degrees of discoloration wchich means different material density and also presence of different substances such as skin oil for example.. Yet you dont seem to care.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no i don't care particularly - you are 100% right the method i use is sloppy and poor practice and the devices would improve significantly if made well. But the point here is to how what can be done with 'kitchen grade' equipment. It is meant to show what you can do if you try
